post.content:
I was asked to design a rate limiter. 
The idea was easy and I implemented TokenBucket Algorithm.
Ask is to design a rate limitter for one single application not on the basis of IP addresess, just incoming requests.

The tough part is this:
1. Api crashes at 1000 request per second. How do I determine the rate at which the requests are prevented. 1000? 800? and why.
2. Api has 1:3 requests for POST and GET. How to determine rate limit with this information.
3. Where to deploy the rate limiter? How to reduce network calls?

Thought it was easy but I messed it up.

Checkout my post on Rate Limiter System. Covers Fixed Window, Sliding Window, Token Bucket and Leaky Bucket implementation:\nhttps://medium.com/@choudharys710/lld-machine-coding-w-implementation-rate-limiter-34f87e74120f

1. Ideally you can use load tests. If that not possible use monitoring history. You want to start droping request before crash. What usually happens is that performance goes down first (latency increases) and then the crash. sd\n2. I am guessing this is prioritizing POSTs vs GETs for given API. So if one has lower priority than the other start droping these requests first and the others only if you still have above limit load. \n3. You can split your bucket per node, so each node gets bucket/nodes limit. This works if your load is split evently across nodes and bucket_size >> no_of_nodes bc of division. Both should be true in most cases. If you nodes cannot handle the same amount of load that you can have a service that would split bucket_size per node. This service would get queried for changes periodicly.

If you need different rate-limiting, depending on whether it is POST or GET request, you may have to deploy 2 different flavors of the same rate limiter which may or may not have different rules. From an Algorithm perspective, you can use a fixed window counter algorithm with a request limit < 1000 for the window. Even for the token buckets, tokens should be less than < 1000 in 1 sec.\n\nYou can deploy it as API gateway if you already have one for some existing requirements e.g. authentication. You can also deploy it as one of the microservice. Network calls can be reduced if you cache the GET result in the cache on the client-side so the client can avoid similar calls.
read this from uber eng blogs. https://www.uber.com/en-IN/blog/cinnamon-auto-tuner-adaptive-concurrency-in-the-wild/?uclick_id=90495edf-c0d0-419e-bc07-eca03ace62fe

1. As mentioned in the post below, run experiments as well as load tests to have a deterministic rate at which requests are dropped. We should also use that information to apply global rate limit on the application. So, if the rate determined after our experiments and load test is 1000 requests / second, we can cap global rate limit at 800.
2. This means that if the rate limit for POST requests is 200 requests / second, GET should be 600 requests / second.
3. We can deploy the rate limiter as part of the API gateway.
4. To reduce network calls, we can cache the GET requests on the client. This however, introduces some complexities. We need to figure out how to ensure that if there is a change in the data, clients will discard their caches and go back to the server to fetch the updated records. If we know the rate at which data changes occur, then we can build it into our implementation. One way to tackle this is to invalidate client cache say every 3 seconds. Each time client has need to serve data, it first checks if the cache is still valid. If the cache is still valid and the data it's looking for is in its cache, it will serve from the cache, otherwise, it will do a network call. 
This approach will generally reduce the number of calls the service receives but also introduces an eventual consistency that is within 3 seconds. Our interviewer should be happy with this. What if we don't have control over the client?
We could resort to adding more workers to help with serving the api requests. We should also apply caching on the server side. While this will generally improve the health of our servers, it does not reduce network calls.
